What is Travelers’ Choice?
Tripadvisor gives a Travelers’ Choice award to accommodations, attractions and restaurants that consistently earn great reviews from travelers and are ranked within the top 10% of properties on Tripadvisor.

See what travelers are saying

  •
    Bruna e Giovanny
    Sao Paulo, SP85 contributions
    WONDERFUL, BEAUTIFUL, SPECTACULAR!!!! Brazilian students pay 7,000 pesos, just present a valid DNE. We managed to go there with two bus companies, easy bus to the terminal near Iguazu and then Uruguay River to the waterfalls, the first bus was 25 reais or 5000 pesos per person and the second 6000 pesos per person. The ticket for the easy bus you buy on the street next to the TTU de foz and the passage of the Uruguay river directly in their cabin near Iguazu in the last house of the terminal. We had a single but with the company easy bus, we warned them that we wanted to go to the waterfalls and they stopped in the middle of a street before the terminal, told us to go down and take a taxi that charged 6000 pesos per person to go to the waterfalls, do not fall into it, go to the terminal and there take the river Uruguay. About the park it is amazing, you can order a map at the entrance, has some spread by the guiches, it has 3 main trails, Devil's Gorge, upper circuit and lower circuit. When you get to the park go straight to get your ticket from the trenzinho to the devil's throat to avoid queues. You can use the ticket at any later time. We recommend starting the visit by the lower and upper circuits and then Devil's throat, in the afternoon it becomes empty and you can enjoy much more. The park has some taps that are said to be drinkable, we do not know if they really were, we are alive, but there are a little dirty but usable bathrooms. It has a very good structure, the trails are suspended and well cared for, on this day we walked 15km throughout the park, if you want to see everything, go ready.
    Visited February 2025
    Traveled as a couple
    Written February 16, 2025
  • Jonas Gunnarsson
    Stockholm, Sweden675 contributions
    There are no falls in the world that can match the magnificence of Iguazu Falls. And the Argentinian side is more breathtaking than the Brazilian side. The overview from the Brazilian side is better, but on the Argentinian side, you are literally in the falls, which is a stunning experience. At our visit (late June 2024), the path to Garganta del Diablo was closed, so we would have to do with the Upper and Lower circuits. Still a fantastic experience, but to experience Garganta del Diablo up close, you would have to go to the Brazilian side or take a boat ride literally into the falls. Also, unfortunately the boat ride to Isla San Martin has been cancelled since 2017. Nevertheless, it is an unforgetable experience, and I recommend a visit to both sides of the falls. A visit to the Brazilian side takes a couple of hours to half a day, whereas visiting the Argentinian side takes more or less a full day. It is fairly easy to get to the Argentinian side from Foz, so there is no real need for an organized tour, unless you are really short on time. Buses depart the Central bus station in Foz (in fact, just north of the actual bus station, there is a stand labelled "Internacional"). If you are a foreigner, you need to get off at the border to be stamped out of Brazil, so you have to tell the bus driver to be dropped off. Otherwise you may end up in trouble. Unfortunately the driver won't wait, so if you have a receipt, you can catch the next bus with the same company. There are two companies, Rio Uruguay and Crucero del Norte. Rio Uruguay seems to have more departures, but I don't know for sure. Hence, choosing Rio Uruguay could mean less waiting time at the Brazilian border. Everyone needs to get off at the Argentinian border and here the bus the driver waits. The bus then goes to the bus station in Puerto Iguazu, Argentina, from which another bus will take you to the falls. There are also buses that go directly to the falls from Foz, without going to the bus stattion, so if you happen to catch one of those, you're in luck. Payment at the park can be done in cash or with a credit card (at an unfavorable exchange rate). Therefore, I recommend that you exchange the Argentinian pesos in Foz, for instance in a large grocery store. Also, food inside the park is, as you would expect, expensive. Hence, consider bringing food and drink for your day in the park.
    Visited June 2024
    Traveled with family
    Written July 13, 2024
  • pps d
    18 contributions
    Practical tips. No online taxi from hotel (we were out of town) so we ended up in a pricey hotel connected outfit at 28USD$ each way. Then again only 10 more than online app. Bought tickets with card at venue, ticket office far left of ATM cabin. Go straight ahead 20metres approx to get free train timed ticket to the intermediate stop for beginning of lower/upper walk. Boarding is 5min before ticket time by carriage number (last number on tkt). All PA is in Spanish. At the next station again on left 20m (ignore meandering crowds) is another ticket office for Devil's throat. We did see people hiking up the first leg which is feasible if you have a long wait for train 1. We arrived at 0900, got a 0910 Train 1 tket and a 0955 T2 ticket. Go back to platform and keep an eye on the guides (unless you know your Spanish). Because, (in our case) once the 0940 had boarded, i spooted a guide speak to platform staff who beckoned his two clients to follow. I showed my later 0955 ticket and was waived through and dirrcted to the few empty seats. So no waiting but no sitting together. upon arrival left is the start, ahead WCs. Lots of level walkways to get to DT very crowded, but people move on and you can wriggle to the front. Limited shade. Hat/water essential. Some had brought along their hotel towels to protect their heads. The guides use parasols. The best photo spot is taken by chaps on ladders selling pictures. I took the view unless they were park staff they have no greater rights than us and briefly paused there and shifted out of view. I was most impressed with an American claiming her right to stand there whilst she selfied. Lots of Spanish words uttered but no attempt to lay on hands. So I reckon we're both right we can stand anywhere. Despite the return time being listed, there were no checks, so it's self board, aim for carriage 1 as it's first. Both and all of the two lower walks are worth it and there's lots of shade and a caged (keeps Lemurs out) food court. The signposted exit is uphill and now in blazing sun. Retracing (not signposted) your steps (if not crowded) is also uphill but on steps and in the shade back to the final train down, or walk down.
    Visited February 2025
    Traveled as a couple
    Written February 22, 2025
  • 1956roland
    Thonex, Switzerland1,260 contributions
    Fall from the Argentine side: see the devil's throat, it's impressive and the must of the visit. To do imperatively, circuit of 2200 meters to go there, partly with small train. The 1200 meter upper circuit is also very good and you can see other waterfalls. The 1700-meter lower circuit, I didn't do it because it was too hot. There is the possibility to take a boat tour approaching the falls duration 2h40 (grand adventure excursion by truck and boat). It is better to see the waterfalls in 2 days or 3 days, that is 1 for Argentina, 1 for Brazil and 1 day to make the boat because we are all wet. fall on the Bresilian side: from the Bresilian side the view is even more imposing than from the Argentine side. The overview is more beautiful, pretty walk under the trees about 1h30. YOU must be there at 08:00, exceptional to do once
    Visited March 2025
    Traveled as a couple
    Written March 29, 2025
  • LesEd
    Kendal, United Kingdom97 contributions
    The falls are fantastic. However, we had real problems getting in. Below is our attempts to get in it took just over two hours from arriving to finally getting in. This problem on our day seemed to be affecting mainly English speaking peoples. Also Mastercard holders. We got the distinct impression the following happens more or less on a daily basis so why haven't the park authorities sorted it out. 1. Both of our credit cards did not work on the prehistoric magnet strip reading card machines. So we were advised to go to the ATM (cash point if English) 2. The ATM was not able to process our cards. 3. Tried a different ticket both still not able to read our cards 4. Went to the Money exchange office an extortionate rate was offered that would have cleaned out our cash. 5. Went to the store that sells snacks and asked if they did cash back on credit cards no. But if we paid in cash they would give us change in Argentine Pesos. Their rate was even more extortionate than the exchange bureau. 6. Nice lady helped us get on the park's website to buy tickets on line. Our bank back in the UK refused the transactions as the line was not secure. 7 Cleaned the magnetic strip with a spectacle wipe as a last attempt. No good. I asked to see the manager. She was absolutely delightful after hearing our tale of woe. Got a ranger to get a car took us to the central hub. He gave us advice on when was the best time to be at certain parts of the park. Everyone we interacted with were very polite and tried to help us. Then came a bit of a slap in the face.. We were feeling a bit frazzled and frustrated and need a coffee and food to help relive our frustrations. Went into the cafe and got some food, low and behold they had modern up to date card readers working off pin and chip or contactless. Fingers crossed dooh mine didn't work, hurrah my wife's did. Apparently my bank had blocked mine because of the number of fail attempts to use it. So they can have the best machines in the food and souvenir outlets but not at the ticket kiosks. You can buy your tickets on line before you go. Make sure you use a secure line.
    Visited March 2025
    Traveled as a couple
    Written April 2, 2025
  • celeini
    La Plata, Argentina20 contributions
    AMAZING, TOWERING, BEAUTIFUL Iguazu Falls is one of the most popular destinations in the province of Misiones in Argentina. They are inside Iguazu National Park a lush biosphere reserve. The walk is divided into several trails, most of which are on foot. To do all the trails included in the visit you have to go early because if they like to travel quietly and / or if they go with unaccustomed children to walk many kilometers it will probably take longer and some tours close from 16:30. A small fact is that on the second consecutive day the entrance leaves half. The entrance to the park includes access to a small train that takes you to the beginning of the different paths. The views of the upper path are beautiful. We went in July, the weather is ideal, cool in the morning and warm in the afternoon. Some advice. Wear comfortable clothing and sports shoes. Repellent: use it even if you don't see mosquitoes, Carry your bottle to hydrate: They can refill their bottles with drinking water at train stations. There are also food courts, where you can shop right there or take your own food. Some of the eating spaces are visited by coaties, if you prefer to avoid contact with those animals there are grilled dining areas to avoid them.
    Visited July 2024
    Traveled with family
    Written July 29, 2024
  • Chaitanya
    Cambridge, Canada1,453 contributions
    The iguazu falls are the main attraction in the city, definitely you want to visit when here. We only saw the Argentina side of the waterfall, but based on feedback from multiple online comments and from talking to a lot of people who visited both, argentina side is the better option. The falls are beautiful and the different trails allow you to get different views of the falls - make sure the ones you wanted to see are available, we got unlucky and missed out on one of the best trails as it was flooded. The boat ride is a definite must do, very good and gets you very wet and right under the falls. Great way to spend a day with family. 1 day is sufficient for the falls - but you do want to get here early, so that you can atleast walk all the 3 main trails.
    Visited November 2023
    Traveled as a couple
    Written August 21, 2024
  • Karolina Z
    Warsaw, Poland76 contributions
    Iguaz? Falls is a must-see. On the Argentinian side, you get up close to the waterfalls with walkways and trails. The best part is the Devil’s Throat, a massive waterfall that roars as it crashes down, you can take the train to get there, it takes about 20 minutes. There are also Upper and Lower trails with different views, plus a jungle path where you might see wildlife like coatis and toucans. The whole trip took us around 5 hours, with some of us doing the boat tour as well. We went it march and it was very very hot, everyone is wet from sweat so make sure you take water and sun protection with you!!!
    Visited March 2025
    Traveled with friends
    Written March 7, 2025
  • Federico L
    Catania, Italy231 contributions
    A unique and exciting experience! Tre percorsi per godersi a pieno questa meraviglia! Go early (closing at 6pm but the various routes close at 4.45pm), comfortable and waterproof shoes. First take the path that takes you to the Garganta del Diablo. If you arrive early you may have more chances to take a nice souvenir photo with fewer people around. You will enjoy the show without a large crowd. Then devote yourself to the other routes. Comfortable walkways (but long, we have traveled about 20km on foot) will lead you near the falls and among unspoilt nature. Eyes open to find, among the nature of this place, the various animals that inhabit it: coti (nice but hungry, so watch out for bags and food), monkeys, toucans, butterflies, parrots and... caimans! If you can and do not want to spend too much bring some fruit or sandwiches, the costs inside the park are quite inflated. Possibility to make excursion with rubber boat (recommended) at an additional cost of 50 € per person. If you go with the family consider that the boat ride is only from 12 years old. Entry price 35,000 pesos (about 23€), discounts for children up to 6 years. The Argentine side, personally, is more beautiful and richer. The Brazilian one offers a complete view of the Argentine Falls but little else. Absolutely recommended, even mandatory if you are in Argentina.
    Visited August 2024
    Traveled with family
    Written August 3, 2024
  • Joan
    Westhill, United Kingdom25 contributions
    Stunning place to visit. Went to both sides of the falls and excellent walkways providing good views of the falls from many angles. Brazilian side was extremely busy though so make sure you have time to see it all. Plenty of wild life around to see too. There are so many separate areas of the falls they are truely spectacular. There is also the opportunity to take a boat trip under the falls if you don’t mind getting absolutely soaked. Gives another perspective of the falls.
    Visited February 2025
    Traveled with friends
    Written February 9, 2025

An amazing place
Apr 2025 • Couples
Worth a trip, the place is amazing, go on the night full moon tour if it's available.
The staff in the park are great and I do recommend staying at the Melia Hotel in the park.

Michael Z
5 contributions
Braziian side better value
Apr 2025 • Family
I find Iguazu transport very expensive! Public bus to Puerto 7.000 pesos.

In Brazilian side I could get an Uber for half the price same distance.

Public busses and ubers less then 1/3 from IguazuArgentina!

I don't understand as Iguazu itself is ok but nothing special.

Park entry Waterfalls 1/4 Argentinian side 4x price brazilian side. Take boat rides from brazilian side to falls much cheaper!
Written April 8, 2025
This review is the subjective opinion of a Tripadvisor member and not of Tripadvisor LLC. Tripadvisor performs checks on reviews as part of our industry-leading trust & safety standards. Read our transparency report to learn more.
Scubaqueen_12
Green Valley, Arizona154 contributions
Great Place to Visit
Mar 2025 • Couples
We loved Iguazu Falls. We visited both the Argentina side and the Brazil side. The views are different and both are very worthwhile. Our Argentina trip was arranged by Knowmad, a small agency from St. Paul, MN, specializing in South America. The guide that they hired was wonderful and stayed with us both days, as did the driver. I would highly recommend them if you are planning a trip to South America and want a personalized, private custom trip. We stayed at the Melia Iguazu and loved it for the convenience and the views.
Written April 7, 2025
This review is the subjective opinion of a Tripadvisor member and not of Tripadvisor LLC. Tripadvisor performs checks on reviews as part of our industry-leading trust & safety standards. Read our transparency report to learn more.

Buy your ticket before you go.
Mar 2025 • Couples
The falls are fantastic. However, we had real problems getting in. Below is our attempts to get in it took just over two hours from arriving to finally getting in.

This problem on our day seemed to be affecting mainly English speaking peoples. Also Mastercard holders. We got the distinct impression the following happens more or less on a daily basis so why haven't the park authorities sorted it out.

1. Both of our credit cards did not work on the prehistoric magnet strip reading card machines. So we were advised to go to the ATM (cash point if English)
2. The ATM was not able to process our cards.
3. Tried a different ticket both still not able to read our cards
4. Went to the Money exchange office an extortionate rate was offered that would have cleaned out our cash.
5. Went to the store that sells snacks and asked if they did cash back on credit cards no. But if we paid in cash they would give us change in Argentine Pesos. Their rate was even more extortionate than the exchange bureau.
6. Nice lady helped us get on the park's website to buy tickets on line. Our bank back in the UK refused the transactions as the line was not secure.
7 Cleaned the magnetic strip with a spectacle wipe as a last attempt. No good.

I asked to see the manager. She was absolutely delightful after hearing our tale of woe. Got a ranger to get a car took us to the central hub. He gave us advice on when was the best time to be at certain parts of the park. Everyone we interacted with were very polite and tried to help us.

Then came a bit of a slap in the face.. We were feeling a bit frazzled and frustrated and need a coffee and food to help relive our frustrations. Went into the cafe and got some food, low and behold they had modern up to date card readers working off pin and chip or contactless. Fingers crossed dooh mine didn't work, hurrah my wife's did. Apparently my bank had blocked mine because of the number of fail attempts to use it.

So they can have the best machines in the food and souvenir outlets but not at the ticket kiosks. You can buy your tickets on line before you go. Make sure you use a secure line.
Written April 2, 2025
This review is the subjective opinion of a Tripadvisor member and not of Tripadvisor LLC. Tripadvisor performs checks on reviews as part of our industry-leading trust & safety standards. Read our transparency report to learn more.
SandySydneyAus
Australia827 contributions
One of a kind
Mar 2025 • Couples
We went on the bus from hotel. Bought return tickets. We started with train to end and train to half way. We walked everywhere. Walkways were good. Ok for all of us over 65. Views were amazing. Good in photos and so also just to stand there to feel.
We did both sides . And enjoyed both.

visitorwithsense
Ivybridge, UK139 contributions
Much better than the Argentine side. Wildlife ++
Mar 2025 • Friends
Visited the Argentinian side yesterday. Very enjoyable and good food but other than a couple of birds no wildlife.
Brazilian side today, no guide but recommend VIP pass. Much more compact trails with excellent views of main falls. The walkway out into the falls is a bit wet but gets superb views and if you do the trail from the bus stop towards the main square you get the option of queuing for the lift back to the square after a mile walk and lots of stairs. Food good and value at the restaurant at the end.
Of the two I would choose the Brazil side as we saw lots of Capochin monkeys with babies and numerous Coaties (walking amongst your feet!)

1956roland
Thonex, Switzerland1,260 contributions
once in a lifetime
Mar 2025 • Couples
Fall from the Argentine side: see the devil's throat, it's impressive and the must of the visit. To do imperatively, circuit of 2200 meters to go there, partly with small train. The 1200 meter upper circuit is also very good and you can see other waterfalls. The 1700-meter lower circuit, I didn't do it because it was too hot. There is the possibility to take a boat tour approaching the falls duration 2h40 (grand adventure excursion by truck and boat). It is better to see the waterfalls in 2 days or 3 days, that is 1 for Argentina, 1 for Brazil and 1 day to make the boat because we are all wet.
fall on the Bresilian side: from the Bresilian side the view is even more imposing than from the Argentine side. The overview is more beautiful, pretty walk under the trees about 1h30. YOU must be there at 08:00, exceptional to do once

A bucket list experience and well worth the visit. The scenery stunning and lots of photo opportunities. My only criticism would be the restaurant the staff were not very friendly. The food is also charged on weight and is expensive for what is served.

gerry p
Braunston, UK21 contributions
Amazing must do experience- some travel advice included
Mar 2025 • Couples
We stayed at the hotel St George on the Argentinian side for two days, then had one day at the Belmond hotel on the Brazilian side. Getting to the falls was easy - there’s a bus every 20mins from the bus station opposite the hotel. Cost is about Ł6 pp each way and the journey takes 25mins. You can buy return tickets. Alternatively, plenty of taxis available, either ordered by the hotel or on the streets. Cost about Ł20 each way, so worth it if more than 2 people compared with the bus. The taxi driver will happily agree to pick you up - just arrange time by WhatsApp once you want to go home.
As for the falls, march seemed a great time to visit.. the park was relatively quiet, but the weather was still good. You have a choice of three main walks.. the devils throat is the must do and if you are only there one day, then the upper circuit is the next best. It’s quite tiring, so two walks in one day was enough - we did the upper + devils throat on the first day, then the lower and repeated devils throat on the second. We also did the full moon tour (see separate review)
It was worth seeing the falls from the Brazilian side, as you see a wide angle view of the complete falls. We stayed in the amazing Belmond hotel (much nicer than the Argentinian equivalent hotel in the park) for one night, but you can easily do it in one day. All the taxi drivers seemed to offer the trip and it was about Ł40 one way and took 50mins across the border..
The falls themselves were staggering and a must do when visiting South America, even though Iguaz? had nothing else to really see or do other than the falls themselves- so no need to stay any longer.

Great walks, well organised food court and lovely train to breath taking waterfalls
Mar 2025 • Family
Well organised, have a lovely train to move around the park, excellent food venues from picnic area to self service to coffee shops and even a fancy churrasco restaurant which we had lunch. The walk to Garganta do Diabo is well organised and take you to great views. Keep a day for this park.
